name: Andrea Pirlo
hints: Won the 2006 World Cup with Italy, playing a key role in midfield throughout the tournament
Won 4 Serie A titles with Juventus after leaving AC Milan on a free transfer in 2011
Was considered surplus to requirements at a Milan club before redefining his career as a deep-lying playmaker
His deep free kick delivery and 'Panenka'-style penalties were signature elements of his game
Became the 'regista' — a position he essentially redefined at the highest level of football

name: Rivaldo
hints: Was the world's best player in 1999, winning both the Ballon d'Or and FIFA World Player of the Year
Scored one of football's most audacious goals — a bicycle kick on the final day of the season to secure Champions League qualification
Won the 2002 World Cup as a key squad member, contributing vital goals in the knockout stages
His time at Barcelona was defined by a complex relationship with the manager who wanted him to play wide
Was fined by FIFA for feigning injury after being touched by a ball in a Champions League qualifier

name: Ronaldo de Assis
hints: Won two consecutive Ballon d'Or awards in 2004 and 2005
Received a standing ovation from Real Madrid fans at the Bernabeu during a league match
Won the 2002 World Cup with Brazil and the 2004/05 Champions League with a Spanish club
Was once banned from Brazil for tax offences, leading to a controversial passport issue
His no-look passes and skill moves were unlike anything European football had seen

name: Riquelme
hints: Considered one of the last great 'enganche' — the traditional Argentine number 10
Led Villarreal to the Champions League semi-final in 2006, scoring the winning penalty in every shootout
Won the Copa Libertadores and Intercontinental Cup with Boca Juniors
Was so dominant at Villarreal that Pep Guardiola reportedly built entire game plans around stopping him
Refused a contract extension at Barcelona under Rijkaard in favour of more game time

name: Steven Gerrard
hints: Produced one of the greatest individual performances in a Champions League final in Istanbul in 2005
Never won the Premier League despite being one of the competition's most dominant midfielders
Notoriously slipped against Chelsea in April 2014, handing the title to Manchester City
Scored an extraordinary long-range FA Cup final goal to take the game to extra time in 2006
A one-club man at Liverpool who declined moves to Chelsea and other clubs to stay loyal

name: Frank Lampard
hints: Chelsea's all-time record scorer with 211 goals — remarkable for a central midfielder
Won the Champions League with Chelsea in 2012, scoring in the semi-final
Was voted FWA Footballer of the Year in 2005 after finishing Ballon d'Or runner-up
Was known for arriving late into the box and timing his runs to perfection
His father Frank Lampard Sr. was also a professional footballer and manager

name: Xabi Alonso
hints: Scored from his own half in a Champions League match — a goal that became iconic
Won the Champions League with two different clubs — Liverpool in 2005 and Real Madrid in 2014
Part of the Spain squad that won the World Cup in 2010 and two European Championships
Won the Bundesliga with Bayern Munich in his final three seasons as a player
Went on to become one of Europe's most coveted managers, leading Bayer Leverkusen to an unbeaten title

name: Gareth Bale
hints: Won four Champions League titles with Real Madrid despite a turbulent relationship with the club
Scored an overhead kick in a Champions League final that is widely regarded as the greatest final goal ever
Led Wales to the European Championship semi-finals in 2016 — the nation's greatest tournament run
Was the world's most expensive player when signed by Real Madrid for £85m in 2013
Started his career as a left back before reinventing himself as a devastating winger

name: Sergio Agüero
hints: Scored the most dramatic goal in Premier League history — a stoppage-time title winner that sparked mass hysteria
Manchester City's all-time record scorer with 260 goals
Won 5 Premier League titles and a Champions League during his time in England
Was the son-in-law of Diego Maradona at one point in his career
Retired from football at 33 after suffering a cardiac episode at FC Barcelona

name: Gerd Müller
hints: Scored 68 international goals for West Germany — a record that stood for 40 years
Won the World Cup in 1974, scoring the winning goal in the final
Nicknamed 'Der Bomber' for his prolific and clinical goal-scoring ability
Won the Ballon d'Or in 1970 and was arguably Europe's best player in the early 1970s
Scored 40 Bundesliga goals in a single season — a record that stood until broken by Lewandowski in 2021

name: Marco van Basten
hints: Scored one of the greatest goals in football history — a volley from an impossible angle in the 1988 European Championship final
Won the Ballon d'Or three times — in 1988, 1989, and 1992
Won the European Cup with AC Milan in 1989 and 1990
His career was ended prematurely at 28 by a chronic ankle injury
Was part of the Dutch team that won the 1988 European Championship alongside Frank Rijkaard and Ruud Gullit

name: Ruud Gullit
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or in 1987 at PSV Eindhoven before joining AC Milan
Captained the Netherlands to the 1988 European Championship — scoring in the final
Won back-to-back European Cups with AC Milan in 1989 and 1990
Was one of the most versatile and complete players of his era — capable in multiple positions
Was a dreadlocked icon whose style on and off the field made him a cultural figure

name: Pelé
hints: The only player in history to win three FIFA World Cups — in 1958, 1962, and 1970
Scored over 1,000 career goals — though many are from unofficial competitions
Won the World Cup at age 17 — the youngest ever winner — in 1958
Was so revered that opposing soldiers reportedly called a ceasefire to watch him play in Nigeria
Was declared a national treasure by the Brazilian government to prevent him from moving abroad

name: Diego Maradona
hints: Scored both the 'Goal of the Century' and the 'Hand of God' goal in the same match at the 1986 World Cup
Won the World Cup with Argentina in 1986, being named the tournament's best player
Transformed an unfashionable Italian club into Serie A champions twice — their only top-flight titles
Was a complex figure whose personal struggles were as legendary as his footballing genius
His number 10 shirt was retired by the Italian club he led to glory

name: Ronaldo Luís Nazário de Lima
hints: Named FIFA World Player of the Year three times — widely considered the greatest number 9 ever
Scored in two separate World Cup finals for Brazil — winning both
His career was interrupted by multiple devastating knee surgeries, yet he kept returning to elite level
Was a youth prodigy who earned a call-up to the national team at 16 without a senior club debut
His movement, pace, and finishing combination has never been matched at the highest level

name: Lothar Matthäus
hints: Won the FIFA World Player of the Year award in 1991 — the first ever recipient
Won the World Cup with West Germany in 1990 as captain
Won the Champions League with Bayern Munich in 2001 at the age of 39
Holds the record for the most World Cup appearances — 25 matches over 5 tournaments
Was equally effective as an attacking midfielder in his prime and a sweeper in his later years

name: Marcel Desailly
hints: Won the Champions League with two different clubs in consecutive seasons — Marseille in 1993 and AC Milan in 1994
Won the World Cup in 1998 and Euro 2000 with France as part of one of the greatest squads ever assembled
Was one of the most physically commanding defenders of his era
Was sent off in the 1998 World Cup final but France still won the match
Moved to Chelsea in 1998 and spent six years in the Premier League

name: Roberto Baggio
hints: Missed the decisive penalty in the 1994 World Cup final shootout — one of the most heartbreaking moments in football
Won the Ballon d'Or and FIFA World Player of the Year in 1993
Carried Italy almost single-handedly through the 1994 World Cup knockout stages
Was transferred from Fiorentina to Juventus, causing protests and riots among Fiorentina fans
Known as 'Il Divin Codino' — The Divine Ponytail — for his iconic hairstyle

name: Ronaldo de Lima II
hints: The only striker to win the World Cup with Brazil whilst at an Italian club
His dribbling and pace in the late 1990s before injuries struck was considered superhuman
Inter Milan reportedly never replaced him — he remains their last truly elite striker
He scored two penalties in two separate World Cup finals for Brazil
Was regarded as the best player in the world from 1996–1998 before knee injuries changed his game

name: Miroslav Klose
hints: The all-time leading scorer in World Cup history with 16 goals across four tournaments
Won the World Cup with Germany in 2014, scoring against Brazil in the famous 7-1 semi-final
Scored 5 goals at the 2002 World Cup to win the Golden Boot in his debut tournament
Known for his trademark somersault goal celebration
Was born in Poland but represented Germany throughout his senior career

name: Ronaldinho Gaucho
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or in 2004 and 2005 during his Barcelona peak
Scored a deliberately lobbed free kick from 35 yards against England in the 2002 World Cup that went in off the crossbar
Was so good that Real Madrid fans applauded him mid-match at the Bernabeu
Won the 2002 World Cup and the 2004/05 Champions League, but never won La Liga
His trademark move involved a feint and flick that left defenders stationary

name: Paolo Maldini
hints: One of the very few players to spend an entire 25-year senior career at a single club
Won the Champions League 5 times and Serie A 7 times — all at the same Italian club
Was never sent off in over 900 career appearances — a near-impossible feat for a top-level defender
His father played for and managed the same club, making them football's most famous dynasty
Was considered the best left back — and later the best centre back — in the world across three separate decades

name: Ryan Giggs
hints: Scored the greatest individual goal in FA Cup history — a mazy run and finish against Arsenal in 1999
Won 13 Premier League titles — more than any other player in the history of the competition
Spent his entire 24-year professional career at a single Manchester club
Was a direct, pacy winger who transformed into a creative, deeper midfielder in his 30s
Won the Champions League twice and played in more Premier League games than anyone else

name: Alessandro Del Piero
hints: Juventus's all-time record scorer and a one-club legend who stayed through relegation
Won the World Cup with Italy in 2006, scoring a crucial late goal against Germany in the semi-final
Has a signature free-kick zone at the edge of the box named 'Del Piero Zone' in Italian football culture
Took a significant pay cut to remain at Juventus after their relegation due to the Calciopoli scandal
Won 6 Serie A titles and reached two Champions League finals with Juventus

name: Thierry Henry
hints: Became the all-time leading scorer for a north London club with 228 goals across two spells
Was part of the 49-game unbeaten side in 2003/04 — the only unbeaten top-flight season in modern English football
Arsene Wenger reinvented him from a winger who struggled at Juventus into one of the greatest strikers in history
Won the World Cup in 1998 and Euro 2000 as a squad player before becoming France's main striker
Scored a controversial handball goal against Ireland in a World Cup play-off that ignited a national scandal
